coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots derived from lychee waste: Application for Fe3+ ions sensing in real water and multicolor cell imaging of skin melanoma cells
coredata.dc:creator: Sahoo, Nandan Kumar
Jana, Gopal Chandra
Aktara, Mt Nasima
Das, Somnath
Nayim, Sk
Patra, Anirudha
Bhattacharjee, Paromita
Bhadra, Kakali
Hossain, Maidul
coredata.dc:description: Exploit of biomass as an inexhaustible resource has accepted much more curiosity to the present research world. Herein, a simple, one-step solvothermal action has been used to synthesize an ascendable amount of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with an average size of~3.13 nm, from Low-reasonable and green source lychee waste. The excitation/emission maxima of CDs have 365/443 nm with high quantum yield (23.5%). The present ingredient predominantly contained carboxylic acid and hydroxyl group that acted as a passive agent for stabilizing the CDs. The structural and optical properties were evaluated through HRTEM, FTIR, UV–vis, zeta potential, XPS, fluorescence, and fluorescence lifetime experiments. We investigated the manoeuvre of our synthesized CDs as a probe for detection of Fe3+ ions in water bodies; This sensing approach showed impressive selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ions with LOD 23.6 nM. The sensing mechanism took place through static quenching which was entrenched through fluorescence lifetime measurements. Fe3+ ions detection was basically carried out with efficacy in real water. For its lofty Photo-stability, low cytotoxicity and cell viability the probe were substantially applied for bio-imaging experiment i.e. intracellular multi-color cell imaging in skin melanoma cells (A375 cells) with and without Fe3+ ions exemplifying its real applications in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Lychee waste
Solvothermal method
Fluorescent carbon dots
Fe3+ ions
A375 cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The interaction of metal ions with CDs
The response of CDs to temperature
Optimum condition of detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis (black line), excitation and Fl. emission (red line and blue line) spectra, inset a, b are the photographic image of CDs in sunlight and under UV lamp, (b) FTIR spectrum of CDs with (red line) and without Fe3+ ions (black line), (c) Zeta potential curve, (d) Excitation dependent Fl. emission spectra. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) TEM image of CDs, insides panel predicts the corresponding size distribution histogram, (b) HRTEM image of CDs, (c) TEM image of CDs + Fe3+ ions (22 μM), (d) HRTEM image of CDs + Fe3+ ions (22 μM).
(a) Entire XPS survey of CDs. High resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1 s.
The Fl. Intensity dependent (a) time (min), (b) NaCl salt (0-300 μM), (c) variation of pH.

(a) Temperature dependent Fl. intensity, (b) temperature dependent emission wavelength at 365 nm excitation, (c) The linearity of normalized Fl. intensity with temperature (°C), (d) Reversibility of Fl. intensity between 20 °C and 80 °C.

(a) Decay curve of CDs (red color) and in presence of Fe3+ ions (green color). (b) Absorption spectra of CDs (black color) and CDs + Fe3+ ions (red color). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Photographic image of CDs with different metal ions under UV light at 365 nm excitation. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with the addition of other metal ions (100 μM) and Fe3+ ion (22 μM). (c) F/Fo of CDs with different metal ions. (d) F/Fo of CDs with different amino acids at 365 nm excitation.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of CDs with various concentration Fe3+ ions (0–22 μM). The inner (a, b) denote the photographic image with and without Fe3+ ions under UV lamp illuminate at 365 nm. (b) Linear relationship between F0/F with various concentrations of Fe3+ ions.
Cell viability of A375 cells in presence of Fe3+ ions (0–1.2 mg/mL).
Microscopic cell image of A375 cells, at bright field, 405 nm, 488 nm, 543 nm, (a) With CDs (b) CDs with 1.6 μM Fe3+ ions, (c) CDs with 22 μM Fe3+ ions. Scale bar is 200 μm.
Schematic representation of CDs preparation from lychee waste and application for Fe3+ ions sensing.
Fluorescence lifetime components of CDs (0.01 mg/mL) obtained with three exponential fit decay curves in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions.
Comparison of CDs derived from various sources for Fe3+ ions detection.

row.entry: Nitrogen and phosphorus codoped carbon nanodots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Fe3+ ions in various water samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots derived from lychee waste: Application for Fe3+ ions sensing in real water and multicolor cell imaging of skin melanoma cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Vidyasagar University, Midnapore 721 102, West Bengal, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Vidyasagar University, Midnapore 721 102, West Bengal, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Zoology, University of Kalyani, Kalyani, 741235, West Bengal, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Zoology, University of Kalyani, Kalyani-741235, West Bengal, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry & Chemical Technology, Vidyasagar University, Midnapore 721 102, West Bengal, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploit of biomass as an inexhaustible resource has accepted much more curiosity to the present research world. Herein, a simple, one-step solvothermal action has been used to synthesize an ascendable amount of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with an average size of~3.13 nm, from Low-reasonable and green source lychee waste. The excitation/emission maxima of CDs have 365/443 nm with high quantum yield (23.5%). The present ingredient predominantly contained carboxylic acid and hydroxyl group that acted as a passive agent for stabilizing the CDs. The structural and optical properties were evaluated through HRTEM, FTIR, UV–vis, zeta potential, XPS, fluorescence, and fluorescence lifetime experiments. We investigated the manoeuvre of our synthesized CDs as a probe for detection of Fe3+ ions in water bodies; This sensing approach showed impressive selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ions with LOD 23.6 nM. The sensing mechanism took place through static quenching which was entrenched through fluorescence lifetime measurements. Fe3+ ions detection was basically carried out with efficacy in real water. For its lofty Photo-stability, low cytotoxicity and cell viability the probe were substantially applied for bio-imaging experiment i.e. intracellular multi-color cell imaging in skin melanoma cells (A375 cells) with and without Fe3+ ions exemplifying its real applications in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: In the changing scenario of rapid industrialization, the hazards caused by heavy metals ions have been posing an alarming problem, and it affected human health as well as the environment. Iron, a key micro-element in living organism exists mainly in the form of Fe2+ (ferrous) and Fe3+ (ferric), they mostly enter in our body through dietary including red meat, cereals fish, beans, leaf vegetable, etc. and play apivotal role in bio-inorganic field i.e. it transports various gas molecules (oxygen, carbon dioxide), transferring electrons and building enzyme (catalase, lipoxygenases) and in deficiency of iron leads to serious health problem mainly anemia disease [1,2] and during pregnancy, the deficiency causes delayed to growth before and after birth of the infant. In human body iron cannot excrete smoothly, only excretes small amount due to mucosal and skin epithelial cell sloughing. So it is too much necessary to control iron up-taking process, excess iron from the normal allowance limit can induce serious disorders and damages tissues, DNA, protein, heart, liver, and overdose iron can causes coma, which eventually causes carcinogenesis. So it is crying need to develop such a swift technique to monitor iron concentration in environment and biological fields,

Up to date, manifold techniques have been employed for detecting Fe3+ ions including atomic absorption spectroscopy, electron paramagnetic resonance, electrochemical, organic dyes, spectrophotometer (such as nanocluster, metal nano, and nano-dots) [3–6], but most of the progression suffers much significant stipulation; for example much more time consuming, sophisticated and knowledgeable instrument are requisite, hard for pre-treatment of the probe, but in contradiction fluorescence nano-materials have growing notably due to its high solubility, simplicity, prompt detection, high specificity, and superb bio-compatibility.

Nano-technology is one of the fascinating technologies which have been extensively used in a number of fields; mainly biomedical and chemical analysis. Admittedly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) area new-fangled category of nanomaterials in carbon families with zero-dimension. Of late the researchers are keen to CDs because of their distinctive thermal, optical, chemical, electronic and mechanical properties, non-blinking luminescence, and magnificent-compatibility, flexibility in surface modification and nominal toxicity which makes CDs appealing fluorescent materials for photo-catalysis [7], cellular imaging [8], medical diagnosis [9,10], photochemical and electrochemical [11], ions detection including Cd2+, Cr3+, Au3+, PO4 3−, Br−/I− [12–16], and various molecules including bio-molecules [17–20]. Most of the CDs have been synthesized through two principal approaches: top-down and bottom-up approaches, including laser ablation [21], arc-discharge [22], thermal decomposition, electrochemical oxidation [23], or oxidation, microwave assisted pyrolysis [24], hydrothermal methods [25], In date, many researchers have developed CDs from various precursor, way, and purposes. Sen Liu et al. prepared CDs from grass through hydrothermal way and successfully used for detection Cu2+ ions [26]. J. He et al. synthesized CDs using citric acid and diethylenetriamine through only one step refluxed method and fruitfully apply for Hg (II) and I− sensor [27]. H. claves et al. used laser ablation way for deriving CDs from NH2-polyethylene-glycol and N-acetyl-L-cysteine and used for detecting Hg2+ions [28]. M. yang et al. developed CDs by refluxing glucose in acidic medium and used detection of Zinc ions in solution and living cells [29]. Also various green sources: like fruits (Carica papaya, carrot, unripe peach, Rose-heart radish, etc.) [30–33], leaves [34], and waste materials (like Onion waste, fish scale, walnut shells, orange waste peels, waste paper, spider silk) [35–40] were used as a source for developing CDs.

But in many cases, some surface passive agents were required for preparation of CDs. Hence, in the realm of nanomaterials it is too much essential to develop such a source which was totally green, cost-effective, eco-friendly and with versatile behaviors. As metal ions play a cardinal role in the environment and biological field, it was necessary to develop a metal sensor with great potentiality in the field of environment and living cells.

In time of investigation about CDs, we recently developed CDs from bio-waste spider silk through hydrothermal way and outstandingly utilized it for nano-molar sensing of Hg2+ ions in the real sample and live cell [40]. For possessing sound physique we maintained a perfect diet and took various fruits, but after eating we threw their bark as waste material. Among these lychee fruit is an essential fruit for common Indian people.

Herein, we proposed an easy one-pot solvothermal economical, green approach for directly synthesizing highly water-dispersible CDs from household waste materials; the bark of ripe lychee fruits without any surface modification or via any passive surface agent. The size, surface charge, surface functional group, and optical properties were analyzed in detail by multiple ultra-modern instruments. The outer surface of CDs was enriched with a high oxygen-rich functional group (hydroxyl and carboxylic acids). The CDs were effectively utilized as a probe for Fe3+ ions detection in a diverse water sample (tap, pond, river) and only 22 μM Fe3+ ions can quench almost 80% of its fluorescence intensity (Fl. intensity) selectively and linear range 0.1–1.6 μM with lod 23.6 nM (Scheme 1 ). Owing to its minimal cell viability and maximal water solubility and the dependence of emission nature on excitation the probe were used for multicolor cell imaging of A375 cell with and without Fe3+ ions.

Ethanol, Rhodamine 6G, was pick up from Merck Pvt. Ltd. (Singapore). To assay the sensing system by various metal ions following compounds were enlisting, NaNO3; KNO3; Al(NO3)3.9H2O; Pb(NO3)2; Mn(NO3)2; Ba(NO3)2; Fe(NO3)3.9H2O; Ca(NO3)2.4H2O; Cd(NO3)2.4H2O; Cu(NO3)2.3H2O; Co(NO3)2.6H2O; Zn(NO3)2.6H2O; Hg(NO3)2.H2O; Ni(NO3)2.6H2O; AgNO3; and all other compounds purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (St. Louis, MO,USA) and were used directly for any further purification as all are in analytical grade. All solutions were made by using de-ionized water.
The absorption spectra of CDs were determined on a Shimadzu UV-1800 Spectro-photometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan) and operated with wavelength range 200–600 nm.
Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured by a Perkin Elmer spectrum (Perkin-Elmer, USA) with a wavelength range 500–4000 cm−1.

All fluorescence measurements were done on Perkin-Elmer LS-55 (USA) Spectro-fluorometer. The width of the excitation slit and emission slit were set to 2.5 mm and 5 mm, respectively, and the emission wavelength was registered from 380 nm to 650 nm ranges.
Zeta potential (ξ) of CDs was measured by taking dynamic light scattering in zetasizer nano ZS instrument (Malvern instrument, Malvern, Worcestershire, UK) equipped with 532 nm wavelengths at 25 °C.
C H N analyses were performed using a Perkin Elmer 2400 CHN Elemental Analyzer.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEM – 2100, HRTEM) images were carried out on a (JEOL Ltd., 1–2, Musashino 3-Chome, Akishima, Tokyo 196–8558, Japan, http://www.jeol.co.jp) and operated at 200 kv. The sample for HRTEM image was ready on a carbon-coated copper grid through deposited one drop CDs solution and subsequently, the copper grid was positioned in a vacuum for dried up the solutions. The average size of the CDs was calculated by analyzing the size of nearer 100 particles through image-J software. The fluorescence lifetime measurement took-place on a Delta Flex-Moduler Fluorescence Life-time system, Horiba Scientific, (UK) employing PBS buffer (pH 7.0) at λex 365 nm λem 443 nm with the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) procedure. The fluorescence lifetime calculation was done by the following equation:
F
t
=
∑
i = 1
n
ai
exp
− t / τi
where the number of segregate decay components was symbolized by n, ai, and τi was indicated an excited state fluorescence life-span and the pre-exponential factors. Correspondingly correlated by means of the ith component (Lakowcz, 2006). The chi-square (χ2) value indicated the best fitting. The consequential data were evaluated by using the inbuild software of this device.
The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy experiment was done using a PHI 5000 Versa probe II scanning XPS microprobe (ULVAC-PHI, U.S.) with monochromatic Al Kα (hυ = 1486.6 eV) radiation.

CDs were derived through a solvothermal way using lychee waste as carbon originator. The crust of ripe lychee fruits was composed of the neighbouring bazaar and its bark were chopped into small fragments. The undersized fragments were subjected to dry under strong sunlight and turned into powder using a mortar. The dust (1.0 g) materials were taken with 10 mL solution (ultrapure water and ethanol) in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and subjected to heat at 180 °C for 5 h. Finally, the brownish-yellow color solution was obtained which further allowed to centrifuged (12,000 rpm, 15 min). After centrifuging the solution, considerably large particle and un-reacted materials were eliminated, and the solution was allowed to dialysis in a dialysis membrane (cut off 3.5 kDa) for 36 h. After all, the light yellow solution was achieved and conserve for further work at 4 °C.

Rhodamine-6G (quantum yield 0.95 at 488 nm in ethyl alcohol medium) was employed as a reference standard to estimation the relative quantum yield of our as-synthesized CDs, and it was considered according to literature procedure [41]. The absolute value of quantum yield was calculated by the following formula.
Q ex
=
Q st
A ex
/
A st
η ex
/
η st
2
where, Qex stands for the quantum yield of the experimental (as-synthesized) CDs, Aex and Ast were the slop of the integrated emission spectra plotted against absorbance of sample and standard, respectively. ηex and ηst indicate the value of the refractive index of the solvent used for dissolving the sample and standard respectively. In time of experiment for both of the samples, excitation Wavelength, gain, and slit band Width were taken the same value. The calculated quantum yield was 23.5%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction of metal ions with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction behavior of various competitive metal ions with CDs was evaluated. Various metal cations (Fe3+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Al3+, Cr3+) with concentration 100 μM mixed with CDs solution and measured its Fl. intensity after 10 min. All experiments were done at normal temperature with wavelength 365 nm. The exceptional interaction behavior, i.e. sensitivity was studied with various concentration of Fe3+ ions (0–22 μM) and observed linearity in quenching incident at 0.1 to1.6 μM, in order to practical purposes the CDs were examined with various real water (pond, tap and river water) sample solutions.

MTT assay was employed to explore its cytotoxicity and bio-compatibility nature. Here A375 (Skin melanoma) cells were taken as a model for cell imaging. The A375 cell was implanted in 96 well culture plates with density (2 × 104 cells) for 24 h, at DMEM medium supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic solution, in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2. After removing the cultural medium, progressively 0–1.2 mg/mL concentration CDs were added to each well and remained for 48 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Finally, the cell was washed with PBS buffered (pH 7.4) and further incubated by MTT (5 mg/mL) with an addition 100 μL DMSO and stay an additional 4 h (37 °C, 5% CO2) and shaking for 10 min (at 37 °C). Moreover, in the end, the absorbance of each well was measured by a micro-plate reader (Emax Precision Micro Plate Reader, Molecular Device, USA).The control examination was done without CDs. The cell viability = Abs CDs/Abs con× 100%. Over all experiments were repeated thrice.

In order to achieve high performance to the biological purpose, we studied the image of A375 cells in multiple wavelength ranges with CDs and different concentration Fe3+ ions. A375 cells were seeded in DMEM medium with 10% (pH 7.0) PBS buffer (5% CO2, 37 °C) for 24 h and additional 6 h after addition of 1.0 mg/mL CDs. For removing the CDs the cells were washed thorley by 10 mM PBS buffer thrice and taken its image on various wavelength by (OLYMPUS IX 70, Olympus Optical Co. Ltd., Sibuya-ku, Tokyo, Japan) microscope. In order to further check the affect of Fe3+ ions in CDs, we added different concentrations of Fe3+ ions and stayed for additional 1 h and take its image by the same instrument on various (mentioned above) wavelength.

The synthesis of CDs involves only by using of lychee waste as a precursor. The prepared CDs were well dispersed in water and form a Yellowish brown color solution. In order to obtain better-quality performance of as-synthesized CDs, the temperature and time of solvothermal carbonization reaction of lychee waste were optimized simply.

At the time of synthesis, to obtain the best optimum condition, we synthesized the CDs at different temperatures (150 °C, 180 °C and 210 °C). All the synthesized CDs solutions were characterized by using UV–vis and fluorescence spectra (Fig. S1-S5). We have examined the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of prepared CDs at above mentioned temperatures, which were shown in Fig. S4 and S5. From Fig. S3, it was observed that only excitation at 365 nm the CDs solutions showed bright luminescence with different fluorescence intensity (which was also evident from the comparable visual exportations when all the respective synthesized CDs solutions were placed under UV lamp with an excitation wavelength of 254 nm, Fig. S2 and from day light observation, Fig. S1), which concluded that 180 °C temperature, was the best fitted temperature for the synthesis of CDs. From the UV–vis curve (Fig. S4), it was observed that the absorption maxima were unchanged for all respective CDs but absorption intensity were different, and from Fig. S5 it was observed that with rising temperature from 150 to 180 °C the emission intensity increases but further rising temperature from 180 to 210 °C the fluorescence intensity decreased. The reason is that at high temperature organic functional groups are ruptured on the surface of the CDs and hence decreases the fluorescence intensity. From the above study we have chosen 180 °C for CDs synthesis.

During the synthesis of CDs, it is vital to make assured that the conversion process of biomass into CDs has reached its completion under the investigational condition before separating the final product. To explore further about this point, the conversion process has been monitored over a period of time at 180 °C and time to time its fluorescence intensity was recorded. The formation of CDs and its fluorescence intensity will be correlating to the concentration of CDs. From the result i.e. kinetic trend (Fig. S6), it was concluded that initially the fluorescence intensity is very low i.e. conversion rate is very poor. Upon increasing time, the conversion rate increases and at 5 h it is maximum. This can be deduced that all convertibles biomass have been converted into CDs, and with further rising the reaction time (8 h), the carbonization conversion rate slightly decreases. This is due to at longer reaction time, the functional groups present on the surface of CDs are decomposed, and hence the fluorescence intensity decreases. After all, the resulting yellowish brown solution was freeze-dried to obtain the carbon dots as powder form (19% mass yield).

The molecular information and morphologies of the CDs were analyzed throughout the multiple spectroscopic experiments like UV–vis, HRTEM, Fluorescence, FT-IR, Zeta potential, C, H, N analyzer and XPS study.

The UV–vis curve (Fig. 1a) of propounding CDs derived from lychee waste display well-built peaks at 332 nm, which may correspond to the n-π* transition of CO group in carboxylic acids present on the surface of CDs.

On elemental analysis, we found 64.90% carbon, 3.28% hydrogen, 1.24% nitrogen and 30.46% oxygen (calculated) which reveals that the synthesized CDs mainly contain carbon-oxygen or oxygen-hydrogen bond containing functional group.

HRTEM experiments (Fig. 2 ) of CDs synthesized from lychee waste, we can see that the CDs were well dispersed i.e. well separated with a diameter range 1.4 to 4.3 and average 3.13 nm nearer 100 random particles were appraised for cunning its size distribution (inset, Fig. 2a), and its lattice fringe was found to be 0.236 nm which corresponds to graphitic lattice [42,43].

FTIR experiment was done to settle on the functional groups that exist on the surface of CDs. Here (Fig. 1b) in FTIR spectrum, we found a broad spectrum at 3067 cm−1which corresponds to OH/N-H stretching bond which may strongly bonded by H-bonding due to may presence of large number carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, the peak at 2981 cm−1 and 2898 cm−1 for CH bonds. The peak present on1565 cm−1 and 1055 cm−1 indicated for the presence of –COOH and CO, bonds. From the above observation, we may deduce that the procured CDs were functionalized with –O-H/N-H, CH, CO, CO, bonds.

Further, the surface element and bond connectivity was analyzed by strongly through XPS study; in Fig. 3a it was strongly recommended that synthesized CDs contains mainly C, O, and N atoms. In Fig. 3a we observed three peaks, 285 eV for C1s, O1s at 532 eV and 399.1 eV for N1 s [44]. The high-resolution scans were recorded, in Fig. 3b (for C1s) and we observed three dissimilar peaks at 283.6, 285.6 and 288.01 eV for C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and CO linkage [45,46]. And the high-resolution curve of O1s (Fig. 3c) exhibits two peaks one at 530.2for CO, and other 534.4 for C-OH/ C-O-C bonds [47,48]. And in Fig. 3d only one distinct peak observed at 399.2 eV which may be attributed for N1 s atoms [49]. From FTIR and XPS study we confirmed the presence of carboxylic acid and hydroxyl group on the outward of CDs.

Surface charge of the well-dispersed CDs were investigated by assessing its Zeta potential value (Fig. 1C) which may also recommend an explanation for a long time stability of CDs, in this experiment we found −32.0 Mev zeta potential, which assigned for the presence of negatively charge acid and hydroxyl groups, and due to the presence of highly hydrophilic hydroxyl and carboxyl group the CDs are highly water dispersible in nature.

To inspect the optical feature of the prepared CDs, the Fl. spectra were recorded with respect to different irradiation time (Fig. 4a), Fl. Intensity on the variance of excitation (Fig. 1d), the presence of an extremely massive concentration of salts (Fig. 4b), various pH of the buffer (Fig. 4c), temperature and also with the concentration of CDs solution (Fig. S7). After synthesized of CDs, we stored at 4 °C for a long time and there was no change on Fl. intensity, and also illuminated under UV lamp long time (7 h) but no change, and from the experiment of its photostability (Fig. 4a and Fig. S8) it was propounds that the prepared CDs possess superior photo-stability. An interesting nature of the CDs was they exhibit variegated emission spectra with discrepancy of excitation, i.e. the fluorescence intensity strongly depends on the excitation wavelength. Fig. 1d showed the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelength ranging from 325 to 425 nm, with the wavelength interval of 10 nm and the fluorescence emission peaks are red-shifted from 426 nm to 502 nm as the excitation wavelength changed from 325 nm to 425 nm and the fluorescence intensity also changed and the maximum emission intensity spectrum was obtained at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Hence we further worked under 365 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. S9 and S10 are the corresponding 3D spectra).

By the experiments (including, the influence of temperature, ionic strength, pH of the solution and concentration of CDs solution) we investigated the behavior of Fl. intensity of CDs. In order to check the influence of salt (NaCl) strength on Fl. intensity, we took various concentration of the salt solution and examined (shown in Fig. 4b). And it was observed that there is no effect even at high ionic strength condition. The pH of solutions is a distinct factor on the emission nature of CDs. Hence, here we examined of Fl. intensity on different pH medium. (Fig. 4c) from the experiments it was observed that at very low or higher pH Fl. intensity is low, and at moderate pH its value is high. This observable fact may be attributed due to, the surface of CDs are contained carboxylic acid and hydroxyl group which is protonated and de-protonated at the minimal and maximum condition and Fl. decreases but at moderate pH, it does not, hence Fl. intensity is high.

The fluorescence intensity has dependent on its concentration (Fig. S7). In general, the fluorescence intensity showed an increment with increasing concentration at low concentration range, but in high concentration the fluorescence intensity decreases, and it is quite common as self-quenching can occur due to higher frequency of molecular collision or due to aggregation of particles and that takes place at higher concentration. Hence at moderate concentrations of CDs are better for its fluorescence properties. In this study, we optimized for maximum intensity and the effect of concentration of CDs on fluorescence intensity was measured by recording emission spectra of the CDs in the concentration range 5 μL to 30 μL. (stock CDs solution in 1 mL PBS buffer). The plot of the emission intensity at 443 nm as a function of the concentration of CDs is shown that the emission intensity increases with increasing the concentration of CDs up to 20 μL and then decreases (Fig. S7). This decrease in intensity may be due to aggregation of particles or the self-quenching process that takes place at higher concentrations. Hence, 20 μL stock solutions were used for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of CDs to temperature
ce:section.ce:para: We also investigated the affect of temperature variant (between 20 °C and 80 °C) on the nature of Fl. intensity at 365 nm excitation (Fig. 5 ). The CDs exhibits variable performance on temperature. Start from 20 °C and when we move towards higher temperature Fl. intensity decreases (Fig. 5a) linearly but the wavelength of emission were not altered (Fig. 5b) and well linearity (Fig. 5c) with R2 = 0.9982, and I0/I = 0.01466 × T + 0.068101, where I0 and I are the Fl. intensity at 20 °C and corresponding elevated temperature. From the outlook of the above research, we suggested that our probe be applied over a broad range of environmental condition. Moreover, it shows a reversible leaning towards temperature variance, i.e. when we increase the temperature from 20 °C to 80 °C and return to the initial state it shows a well reproducibility, hence our probe does not destruct permanently, (Fig. 5d) i.e. it may use as temperature sensors on various fields.

To detected Fe3+ ions, the entire process was done thrice to attain tangible result at room temperature using 10 mM PBS buffer solution (pH 7.0). In a typical run, 20 μL of CDs (0.01 mg /mL) was added to 1.0 mL PBS buffer for dilution, and Fl. intensity was considered at 365 nm wavelength. Then the diluted CDs solution was titrated against the sequential addition of Fe3+ (0–22 μM). The terminating mixture with in cell was left to stand for 5 min and permit to measure its Fl. intensity at 365 nm excitation. All the spectra were measured within 380–650 nm range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimum condition of detection of Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: To gate better dealing of quenching arrangement we optimized its various components (e.g. reaction time, pH, salt concentrations) which have an impact on the sensing system, shown in Fig. S11, S12, and S13, from the figure (Which represented the experimental results) it was observed that reaction time are not a factor on quenching incident, and also we did in the presence of an utmost concentration of salt but there was no affect on quenching process. From Fig. S12 it was noticed that at pH 7.0 the reaction happened with more acceptable results and hence sensing experiment was done on pH 7.0 (details explain on supply).

Many satisfying agencies can be listed for fluorescence quenching at the time of mechanism investigation with the addition of Fe3+ ions. Generally, the quenching begins from the formation of complex (static mechanism) or electron transfer from CDs to Fe3+ (dynamic).

Some researchers established their concept through some root; H. Liu et al. developed CDs from Bombyx-mori silk and reported that the fluorescence was quenched by Fe3+ ions through aggregation i.e. static mechanism [50]. Z. Xie et al. synthesized CDs from citric acid and 1-aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium through hydrothermally and explained that hydroxyl group of CDs surface chelate with Fe3+ ions leads quench the fluorescence [51]. J. Yu et al. formed CDs using L-glutamic acid as a precursor through the hydrothermal method and explain their incident of fluorescence quenching through dynamic path way i.e. electron transfer occurred NCDs to Fe3+ ions [52]. W. Liu et al. synthesized CDs from rose-heart radish through one-pot hydrothermal way and explain that fluorescence intensity decreases in the presence of Fe3+ due to chelating and electron transfer way (both static and dynamic) [33].

Herein, the surfaces of our prepared CDs are covered by enormous number of oxygen containing functional groups particularly carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. With addition of Fe3+ ions to that of the synthesized CDs significant amount of fluorescence intensity decreases that may be happened by two processes either by chelating Fe3+ ions with the surface of CDs or by energy transfer from surface groups to d-orbital of metal atoms. Here it is important to note that our synthesized CDs have various surface functional groups including hydroxyl moieties and Fe3+ was known to be able to adsorb on the surface of CDs and coordinate with hydroxyl group on the edge of carbon dots. Fe3+ ions have high affinity to these oxygenated functional groups of CDs and formed a complex resulting the aggregation of CDs which is non-radiative, net results fluorescence quenching. Owing to this complex formation, may the electrons were transferred from excited state of CDs to the d-orbital of Fe3+ ions and showed quenched phenomenon. But from the results of our TSCPS measurements, the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was not significantly change upon addition of Fe3+, hence we conclude that CDs formed non-radiative aggregation and hence fluorescence quenching results.

Here, we established a scheme, where among all metals ions only Fe3+ ions can quench fluorescence intensity. To build sour propound mechanism we performed following multiple experiments, HRTEM, FTIR, Zeta potential, UV–vis and fluorescence lifetime.

From the experiment by HRTEM (Fig. 2), we conceivably assess the size of the synthesized CDs (Fig. 2a) moreover, here we also evaluated the size of the CDs after addition of Fe3+ions (Fig. 2C) and we observed that the average size of the formulated CDs increases, which incident may happen due to formation of aggregation and the lattice fringe of CDs before and after addition of Fe3+ are closer, it may happen the interior properties of lattice was not affected by the addition of ions, only ions bind the outer surface.

In the FTIR experiment with and without Fe3+ions (Fig. 1b) we observed upon addition of ions the IR band shifted to the lower region which might be occurred due to the formation of a complex with the Fe3+ ions and functional groups present in the surface of CDs.

The zeta-potential value of prepared CDs in addition of Fe3+ ions changes from higher negative to lower negative value which may also attribute to the positive ions adsorbed the outer surface of CDs and negative charges decreases. From the result, it may be concluded that a complex is formed between ions and CDs (Fig. S14).

Moreover, to further demonstrate the particular mechanistic way i.e. the process occurred through static or dynamic, we performed fluorescence lifetime experiment using a TCSPC technique. The free decay curve and with Fe3+ ions were well fitted with tri-exponential decay kinetics (Fig. 6a). the lifetime components are 0.734 ns (30%), 3.0 ns (34%), 8.78 ns (36%) with an average lifetime 4.40 ns and after addition of Fe3+ ions the components were changes to 0.666 ns (29%), 2.75 ns (34%), 8.51 ns (37%) with an average 4.28 ns lifetime (Demonstrated in Table 1 ). From the above average life-time value it observed that the lifetime value does not change markedly i.e. it forms only complex with added ions but not electron transfer is done. From the above all experiment we concluded that in our proposed research scheme the metal ions quench the fluorescence intensity through the static mechanism.

The time resolved Stern-Volmer equation was as follows Eo/E = 1 + Ks [Q]: where Eo and E are the standards and the average lifetime of free carbon dots and with Fe3+ (quencher). And the three exponential function equation was: (t) = Σ?? 3 = ai exp(− t/τi)F, (t) was the normalized fluorescence decay, a1, a2 and a3 were the normalized amplitude of the decay component τ1, τ2, and τ3. Average lifetime was obtained from the equation. (τ) = Σaiτi.

In time of UV–vis experiment of CDs with all of the ions, we observed that absorption increases only in the presence of Fe3+ ions without any blue or red shift (Fig. 6b, Fig. S15). This may happen due to formation of a complex with CDs and ions. It was extra evidence in favour of static quenching mechanism.

To explore the specificity of our projected sensing method, the Fl. Quenching interpretation were done in presence of different metals ions including Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Al3+, at the concentration of 100 μM which is five-fold higher to that of Fe3+ ions concentration. The variance of Fl. intensity was studied at 365 nm. Different incident was observed with a variety of metal ions and were analyzed crucially. It was consciousness that among all of the verified metal ions only Fe3+ rendered memorable feedback, but others alien ions had no more additional effect, which distinctly indicates that our proposed CDs were trustworthily used as a sensing platform to detect Fe3+ions (Fig. 7a-d). We also examined the sensitivity test by taking far higher concentrations (200 and 300 μM) of other competitive ions with respect to Fe3+ ions (Fig. S16). From the results it was observed that even at far higher concentration of others ions, the quenching affect was not significant; hence we concluded that our proposed system is highly specific towards Fe3+ ions compared to high concentration others ions.

The sensitivity of the proposed method was quantified by a titration experiment with a variation of Fe3+ ions concentration to get a palpable idea. With restrained addition of Fe3+ ions (0–22 μM) to the CDs, Fl. intensity decreases and holds good linearity between the concentration of Fe3+ ions and quenching efficiency (F0/F) and obtained linearity from 0.1–1.6 μM under optimum condition (Fig. 8a-b) The above observation exposed that the CDs bound Fe3+ fruitfully and affects its fl. Intensity. The detection limit, according to IUPAC convention (3σ/k) was found to 23.6 nM. (Details were explained in supplementary section) which is superior to many other research studies (comparison Table 2 ) [33,35,50,53–59] with correlation efficient R2 = 0.9998 this proposed system showed very high specificity towards Fe3+ ions. From the comparison table, we concluded that our system offers a number of advantages from LOD, linear range, and relative quenching capability.

Furthermore, we have studied the fluorescence intensity of the CDs upon addition of Fe3+ ions in the presence of all other metal ions (100 μM conc. of each metals ions) and without Fe3+ ions in the same sample (Fig. S17). Indeed, the fluorescent intensities of CDs was remarkably quenched only in the presence of Fe3+ ions, whereas no significant fluorescence quenching was noticed with other environmentally competing chemical species without Fe3+ ions. These results revealed that the quenching capability of Fe3+ ions in presence of others ions are intact. Hence, we concluded that our proposed method is strictly applicable to detection Fe3+ ions in presence of other coexisting ions.

We also survey its sensitivity nature by various amino acids including Gly, L-cys, L-ser, Ala, Trp, His, Tyr, Pro, Val, Met, shown in Fig. 7d, the interferences ability of the experimental amino acids are negligible. From the observation, we strictly mentioned that our proposed probe does not bind with the experimental amino acids i.e. it is highly selective towards Fe3+ ions.

To inquire about the powerful affinity of CDs towards Fe3+ ions; we have performed an assessment with other iron-containing ions, like Fe2+, [Feii(CN)]4−, [Feiii(CN)]3−. Hemoglobin (Hb, contains Fe2+ions) [Fig. S18], from the results we observed that the moiety or compound don't interact with CDs, hence Fl. intensity was not quenched.

Hence we culminate that our system selectively binds only Fe3+ ions among all of the species containing iron atoms with LOD 23.6 nM, which was much lower than the threshold limit (0.54 μM/L), according to the U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA).

We have inspected the sensing behaviour of our delineate method to real sample, i.e. real water sample collected from disparate provenance (Pond water, tap water, river water). Primarily all the collected samples were filtered through a qualitative filter paper of 0.22 microporous. The filter was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. to remove large particles the standard addition method was used to determine the unknown concentration of Fe3+ ions in the water samples. The Fl. intensity of the CDs decreased when the sample had been spiked with a standard solution.
The recovery =
C 2
−
C 1
/
C 0
× 100 %
where, C2 is the concentration of Fe3+ after the addition of standard Fe3+. C1 are the concentration before the addition of standard Fe3+ ions, C0 is the concentration of Fe3+ added in the real water sample, above equations considered for calculating the recoveries of the real samples. The results were discussed in Table 3 .the recoveries of all sample lie 97.20 to 103.70 and RSD 1.7 to 3.2 these results are good accuracy and applicability towards Fe3+ ions detection in real sample.

Many researchers were grown and apply CDs to cell imaging purposes, i.e. for potential bio-application. Here we assess the potential utility of our synthesized CDs for cell imaging of A375 cell. Firstly, we conduct an inquiry to its cytotoxicity effect on A375 cell through MTT assay. MTT assay was carried out by A375 cells in the presence of CDs which were shown in (Fig. 9 ) from the results reveal that although after addition of large concentration CDs, the cell viability permitted more than 89% of A375 cells. Hence the CDs are addressed potentially for living cell imaging and others bio-medicinal application.

Here, we used A375 cells to explore multi-color cell imaging by up-taking the microscopic image of A375, which depicted in Fig. 10 . Fig. 10 (a, b and c) are the microscopic image of A375 cell under bright field, excitation at 405 nm, 488 nm, and 543 nm wavelength for 12 h duration. After the addition of Fe3+ ions of 1.6 μM (b series) and 22 μM (c series) concentrations, the shining of Fl. intensity reduced. Hence our proposed CDs can apply prolifically to multi-color cell image in presence and absence of Fe3+ ions.

In conclusion, we introduced an effort-less, cost-effective and green approach for developing lofty water soluble CDs with quantum yield 23.5%. Synthesized CDs are high luminescent which depends upon excitation, and the said CDs have incredible photo-stability, superb cell viability, and low cytotoxicity. More consistently the CDs prepared from lychee waste without having chemical reagents i.e. with-out any surface modification. The outer surface of derived CDs contained mainly carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups (oxygen-rich) leading a nano-molar (LOD 23.6 nM) sensor of Fe3+ ions. It is postulated that the sensing system mediated Fe3+moves through a static mechanism which is confirmed by TSCPS measurement. As its emission phenomena depend on excitation, and assignable to its low cell viability, it is effectively applied for multicolor cell imaging. Additionally, this designed chemo-sensor offers multiple advantages; i.e. (i) CDs derived from a green source, lychee waste as carbon source with-out any chemicals. (ii) Highly photo-stable (iii) effective probe for nano-molar sensing of Fe3+ ions only through the mix and detect process within a minute.(iv) A productive platform for multicolor cell imaging of A375 cell. Further-more we conclude that our entire scheme unveils new avenues to the advanced researcher for developing cost-effective green source nano-particle for sensors in the environmental and biological system.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Of India, No. SB/FT/CS-051/2013 (SERB/F/3694/2015-2016). Mt. Nasima Aktara is supported by Senior Research Fellowships, University Grants Commission, Government of India awarded through MANF. Departmental instrumental facilities from DST-FIST and UGC-SAP programs are gratefully acknowledged. We gratefully acknowledge the help provided by USIC, Vidyasagar University for doing spectroscopic measurements.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of N-doped micropore carbon quantum dots with high quantum yield and dual-wavelength photoluminescence emission from biomass for cellular imaging
Nonmaterials

Molecules

J. Nanopart. Res.
